**Alan Belcher** (born April 24, 1984) is an American [bare-knuckle boxer](/wiki/Bare-knuckle_boxing "Bare-knuckle boxing") and former [mixed martial artist](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"). A professional from 2004 until 2015, he is perhaps best known for his 15-fight tenure with the [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC"). He currently competes in [Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") where he was the [BKFC Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#Heavyweight_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Belcher was born in [Jonesboro, Arkansas](/wiki/Jonesboro,_Arkansas "Jonesboro, Arkansas") and began practicing [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate") when he was eight years old.[[2]](#cite_note-ao11-2) After starting [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") at the age of 14, Belcher began his amateur mixed martial arts career at the age of 15.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Belcher graduated from [Sloan-Hendrix High School](/wiki/Sloan-Hendrix_High_School "Sloan-Hendrix High School") in [Imboden, Arkansas](/wiki/Imboden,_Arkansas "Imboden, Arkansas") where he competed in [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), before continuing his baseball career at [Dyersburg State Community College](/wiki/Dyersburg_State_Community_College "Dyersburg State Community College").[[2]](#cite_note-ao11-2) After two semesters, he dropped out in pursuit of a career in mixed martial arts.[[2]](#cite_note-ao11-2)

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

Belcher made his professional debut in July 2004 against Tim Ellis in Freestyle Fighting Championship and won by [TKO](/wiki/Knockout#Technical_knockout "Knockout"). His MMA career then picked up significantly in early 2006, the year in which Belcher amassed 9 fights, going 8–1.

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

In 2006 he lost a unanimous decision at the hands of [Yushin Okami](/wiki/Yushin_Okami "Yushin Okami") at [UFC 62](/wiki/UFC_62 "UFC 62") in his UFC debut.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Belcher made his second UFC appearance against [Jorge Santiago](/wiki/Jorge_Santiago "Jorge Santiago") three months later at [UFC Fight Night: Sanchez vs. Riggs](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night:_Sanchez_vs._Riggs "UFC Fight Night: Sanchez vs. Riggs"), winning by stunning head kick knockout in the third round of a close fight.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

At [UFC 69](/wiki/UFC_69 "UFC 69"), Belcher lost to *[The Ultimate Fighter 3](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_3 "The Ultimate Fighter 3")* winner [Kendall Grove](/wiki/Kendall_Grove "Kendall Grove") via D'arce choke.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

One month later, Belcher stepped in on short notice to replace injured [Eric Schafer](/wiki/Eric_Schafer "Eric Schafer") against [Sean Salmon](/wiki/Sean_Salmon "Sean Salmon") at [UFC 71](/wiki/UFC_71 "UFC 71") on May 26, 2007.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Belcher won the bout via guillotine choke 53 seconds into the first round.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Following the bout, Belcher signed a new four-fight contract with the UFC.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Belcher then faced [Kalib Starnes](/wiki/Kalib_Starnes "Kalib Starnes") at [UFC 77](/wiki/UFC_77 "UFC 77") on October 20, 2007.[[12]](#cite_note-12) After controlling the fight with punches and knees from the [clinch](/wiki/Clinch_fighting "Clinch fighting"), the fight was eventually stopped in favor of Belcher due to a large gash over Starnes' right eye.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Belcher was scheduled to fight at [UFC 81](/wiki/UFC_81 "UFC 81") against [Ricardo Almeida](/wiki/Ricardo_Almeida "Ricardo Almeida"), but Belcher had to pull out two days before the fight due to a severe case of [bronchitis](/wiki/Bronchitis "Bronchitis") and was replaced by Rob Yundt.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Belcher was scheduled to fight [Patrick Côté](/wiki/Patrick_C%C3%B4t%C3%A9_(fighter) "Patrick Côté (fighter)") at [UFC 83](/wiki/UFC_83 "UFC 83") in Montreal, but Côté withdrew due to a knee injury and was replaced by [Jason Day](/wiki/Jason_Day_(fighter) "Jason Day (fighter)").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Belcher eventually was caught by a punch causing Day to swarm punches at Belcher against the fence. The fight was ruled a [TKO](/wiki/Knockout#Technical_knockout "Knockout") in favor of Day in the first round.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

After a controversial split decision victory over [Ed Herman](/wiki/Ed_Herman_(mixed_martial_artist) "Ed Herman (mixed martial artist)"), Belcher fought [Pride FC](/wiki/Pride_FC "Pride FC") veteran [Denis Kang](/wiki/Denis_Kang "Denis Kang") at [UFC 93](/wiki/UFC_93 "UFC 93").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Kang won the first round after taking down and controlling Belcher on the ground. However, Kang began to fight more and more conservatively, and when he tried to take Belcher down again late in the second round, Belcher caught him in a tight [guillotine choke](/wiki/Guillotine_choke "Guillotine choke") that made Kang almost immediately tap out, winning him a $40,000 *Submission of the Night* bonus.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-ufc93b-23)

Belcher lost a split decision to [Yoshihiro Akiyama](/wiki/Yoshihiro_Akiyama "Yoshihiro Akiyama"), at *[UFC 100](/wiki/UFC_100 "UFC 100")* on July 11, 2009.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The fight earned *Fight of the Night* honors, rewarding both fighters with a record setting $100,000.[[25]](#cite_note-ufc100b-25)

Belcher defeated [Wilson Gouveia](/wiki/Wilson_Gouveia "Wilson Gouveia") via first round [TKO](/wiki/Knockout#Technical_knockout "Knockout") on December 12, 2009, at [UFC 107](/wiki/UFC_107 "UFC 107").[[26]](#cite_note-26) During the fight, Belcher displayed a good chin and precise punches. The fight was awarded *Fight of the Night* which rewarded both fighters with $65,000.[[27]](#cite_note-ufc107b-27)

Belcher defeated [Patrick Côté](/wiki/Patrick_C%C3%B4t%C3%A9_(fighter) "Patrick Côté (fighter)") on May 8, 2010, at [UFC 113](/wiki/UFC_113 "UFC 113") by way of [rear naked choke](/wiki/Rear_naked_choke "Rear naked choke") after a controversial face plant that rocked Cote and enabled Belcher to sneak around to the back and sink in the choke.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Belcher received an extra $65,000 for winning the *Submission of the Night* award which was his fourth consecutive fight night bonus.[[29]](#cite_note-ufc113b-29)

Belcher was expected to face [Demian Maia](/wiki/Demian_Maia "Demian Maia") on September 15, 2010, at [UFC Fight Night: Maia vs. Belcher](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night:_Maia_vs._Belcher "UFC Fight Night: Maia vs. Belcher") in his first main event.[[30]](#cite_note-30) However, on August 1, 2010, Belcher withdrew from the fight with Maia after undergoing emergency eye surgery. Alan stated on his [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") account that he started to lose the vision in his right eye whilst in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Belcher's manager, Malki Kawa, in April updated his recovery, saying that Belcher was back in training and looking forward to a return to the Octagon in September.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Belcher faced [Jason MacDonald](/wiki/Jason_MacDonald "Jason MacDonald") on September 17, 2011, at [UFC Fight Night 25](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_25 "UFC Fight Night 25").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Belcher stuffed a takedown from MacDonald and ended up on top early in the first round. Belcher quickly postured up and began landing punches and elbows to open Jason's guard. He then passed and continued to punish MacDonald and won the fight via verbal submission due to strikes in the first round.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Belcher next faced [Rousimar Palhares](/wiki/Rousimar_Palhares "Rousimar Palhares") on May 5, 2012, at [UFC on Fox 3](/wiki/UFC_on_Fox_3 "UFC on Fox 3").[[35]](#cite_note-35) Despite getting the fight to the ground, Palhares was unable to complete his attempted [leg locks](/wiki/Leg_lock "Leg lock") and [heel hook](/wiki/Leglock "Leglock") submissions, and Belcher even attempted a few submissions of his own. Belcher would go on to defeat Palhares via [TKO](/wiki/Knockout#Technical_knockout "Knockout") in the first round.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Belcher was expected to face [Vitor Belfort](/wiki/Vitor_Belfort "Vitor Belfort") on October 13, 2012, at [UFC 153](/wiki/UFC_153 "UFC 153").[[37]](#cite_note-37) However, Belfort was pulled from the bout to face [Jon Jones](/wiki/Jon_Jones "Jon Jones") on September 22, 2012, at [UFC 152](/wiki/UFC_152 "UFC 152").[[38]](#cite_note-38) Belcher called the collapse of the [UFC 151](/wiki/UFC_151 "UFC 151") a "blessing in disguise" as he would have been unable to compete due to a fractured spine, keeping him out of action until November. He wanted to face [Chris Weidman](/wiki/Chris_Weidman "Chris Weidman") in his return fight.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

A rematch with [Yushin Okami](/wiki/Yushin_Okami "Yushin Okami") took place on December 29, 2012, at [UFC 155](/wiki/UFC_155 "UFC 155").[[40]](#cite_note-40) Belcher lost the bout by unanimous decision.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Belcher faced [Michael Bisping](/wiki/Michael_Bisping "Michael Bisping") on April 27, 2013, at [UFC 159](/wiki/UFC_159 "UFC 159").[[42]](#cite_note-42) Bisping controlled the majority of the fight due to his conditioning and cardio pushing a constant pace. The bout was stopped at 4:29 of round 3 as Bisping inadvertently poked Belcher in the eye, rendering Belcher unable to continue. Bisping won the bout via technical unanimous decision.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Belcher got eight stitches in his eyelid after the fight.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

On November 11, 2015, Belcher announced his retirement from mixed martial arts.[[45]](#cite_note-45) A few years afterwards, Belcher negotiated a release from his prevailing UFC contract.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Return from retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Return from retirement")]

Belcher was set to return from retirement to face Marcus Sursa at Empire FC 5 on February 29, 2020, but the bout never materialized for unknown reasons.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Gamebred Bareknuckle MMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Gamebred Bareknuckle MMA")]

Belcher faced [The Ultimate Fighter: Heavyweights](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter:_Heavyweights "The Ultimate Fighter: Heavyweights") tournament winner [Roy Nelson](/wiki/Roy_Nelson_(fighter) "Roy Nelson (fighter)") at Gamebred Bareknuckle MMA 6 on November 10, 2023.[[48]](#cite_note-48) He won the fight via split decision.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Belcher faced former [UFC Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/UFC_Heavyweight_Champion "UFC Heavyweight Champion") [Junior dos Santos](/wiki/Junior_dos_Santos "Junior dos Santos") in the main event of *Gamebred Bareknuckle MMA 7* for the inaugural Bareknuckle MMA Heavyweight Championship on March 2, 2024.[[50]](#cite_note-gamebredmmabk-50) Belcher lost by knockout in the second round.[[51]](#cite_note-GBMMA7-51)

### Global Fight League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Global Fight League")]

On December 11, 2024, it was announced that Belcher was signed by Global Fight League.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Belcher was scheduled to face Stuart Austin on May 25, 2025 at GFL 2.[[53]](#cite_note-53) However, all GFL events were cancelled indefinitely.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Karate Combat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Karate Combat")]

Belcher competed in the 8-man heavyweight tournament July 18, 2025 at *Karate Combat 55* and lost to Zac Pauga by technical knockout in the first round.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Bare-knuckle boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Bare-knuckle boxing")]

On May 20, 2021 – over five years removed from his MMA retirement and nursing the eye injury – it was announced that Belcher had signed a multi-fight contract with [Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship](/wiki/Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship "Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Belcher made his debut against Tony Lopez on August 20, 2021, at [BKFC 20.](/wiki/2021_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_20:_Bedford_vs._Barnett "2021 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship")[[57]](#cite_note-57) He won the fight by unanimous decision.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

He made his sophomore appearance in the sport against Bobo O'Bannon at [BKFC Fight Night 5](/wiki/BKFC_Fight_Night_5 "BKFC Fight Night 5") on January 29, 2022.[[59]](#cite_note-59) He won the bout via first-round knockout.[[60]](#cite_note-60) This fight earned him the *Knockout of the Night* award.[[61]](#cite_note-bkfcjack-61)

He then faced Frank Tate at [BKFC Fight Night 9](/wiki/BKFC_Fight_Night_9 "BKFC Fight Night 9") on June 11, 2022. He won the bout via first-round knockout.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This fight earned him another *Knockout of the Night* award.[[63]](#cite_note-bkfcjackson2-63)

### BKFC Heavyweight Championship reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: BKFC Heavyweight Championship reign")]

Racking three straight wins in the division, Belcher challenged the reigning champion Arnold Adams for the BKFC Heavyweight Championship at [BKFC 36](/wiki/2023_in_Bare_Knuckle_Fighting_Championship#BKFC_36_New_Orleans:_Adams_vs._Belcher "2023 in Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship") on February 24, 2023.[[64]](#cite_note-64) He claimed the title via third-round knockout.[[65]](#cite_note-65) After Belcher booked his [professional boxing bout](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing") against Chase DeMoor, he was stripped of his BKFC Heavyweight title but still continues to be a part of the organization's roster.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Boxing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Boxing career")]

### Belcher vs. DeMoor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Belcher vs. DeMoor")]

Belcher was originally scheduled to face [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing") [Hasim Rahman Jr.](/wiki/Hasim_Rahman_Jr. "Hasim Rahman Jr.") on the undercard of [MF & DAZN: X Series 008](/wiki/NichLmao_vs._Swarmz_vs._BDave_vs._Ryan_Johnston "NichLmao vs. Swarmz vs. BDave vs. Ryan Johnston") on July 22, 2023 at the [Nashville Municipal Auditorium](/wiki/Nashville_Municipal_Auditorium "Nashville Municipal Auditorium") in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee").[[67]](#cite_note-67) However, due to an injury, Rahman withdrew leaving Detrailous "Teddy" Webster as a late replacement. Webster failed his medicals thus resulting in [reality television](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television") star, [Chase DeMoor](/wiki/Chase_DeMoor "Chase DeMoor"), stepping in as a replacement for an [exhibition bout](/wiki/Exhibition_fight "Exhibition fight").[[68]](#cite_note-68) On the night, Belcher defeated DeMoor via 3rd round TKO.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Belcher&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Belcher has a daughter and a son from a previous marriage.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)[[70]](#cite_note-70) He currently trains an American Top Team MMA Gym located in [D'Iberville, Mississippi](/wiki/D%27Iberville,_Mississippi "D'Iberville, Mississippi").
